name: typescript-advanced-types
description: Master TypeScript's advanced type system including generics, conditional types, mapped types, template literals, and utility types for building type-safe applications. Use when implementing complex type logic, creating reusable type utilities, or ensuring compile-time type safety in TypeScript projects.
capabilities: Implement complex generic types with constraints and inference
Create conditional types for dynamic type logic
Build mapped types for property transformations
Use template literal types for string pattern matching
Apply utility types like Partial, Pick, Omit, and Record
Design type-safe event emitters and API clients
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer detected 76 patterns but all are false positives. The skill contains documentation-only content with TypeScript code examples in markdown format. No executable code, network calls, or security risks present. All detected patterns are from code snippets used for educational purposes.
valueStatement: Build type-safe applications with confidence using TypeScript's powerful type system. Learn generics, conditional types, mapped types, and utility types through practical examples.
limitations: Focuses on TypeScript type system, not runtime behavior
Requires existing TypeScript knowledge for advanced concepts
Does not cover JavaScript-to-TypeScript migration strategies in depth
Examples are educational and may need adaptation for production use

useCases.title: Type-Safe Library Development
useCases.description: Build reusable libraries and frameworks with strong type guarantees that provide excellent developer experience.

useCases.title: Complex API Client Design
useCases.description: Create type-safe API clients with automatic inference of request parameters, response types, and error handling.

useCases.title: Enterprise Application Architecture
useCases.description: Implement robust type systems for large-scale applications with complex data models and state management.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Generic Function
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a generic function that accepts any type and returns the same type. Show how TypeScript infers the type automatically and how to explicitly specify it.

promptTemplates.title: Conditional Type for Validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a conditional type that checks if a type extends another type. Use it to create a type-level validation system for common data types like strings, numbers, and arrays.

promptTemplates.title: Mapped Type for API Responses
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a mapped type that transforms all properties of an API response type to be optional and readonly. Include key remapping to add getter methods.

promptTemplates.title: Type-Safe Event System
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement a complete type-safe event emitter using generics, conditional types, and template literal types. Support multiple event types with their specific payload types.

bestPractices: Use unknown instead of any to enforce type checking before operations
Prefer interface for object shapes and type for unions and complex types
Leverage TypeScript's type inference and avoid unnecessary type annotations
antiPatterns: Overusing any type which defeats TypeScript's type safety guarantees
Creating overly complex types that slow down compilation and reduce readability
Ignoring strict null checks which can lead to runtime errors in production

faq.question: What is the difference between interface and type in TypeScript?
faq.answer: Use interface for object shapes with better error messages and declaration merging. Use type for unions, intersections, and complex type logic. Both can describe object shapes, but interfaces offer better extensibility.

faq.question: When should I use generics versus union types?
faq.answer: Use generics when you need type flexibility while maintaining relationships between input and output types. Use union types when a value can be one of several specific types without type relationships.

faq.question: How do conditional types work in TypeScript?
faq.answer: Conditional types use the extends keyword with a ternary-like syntax: T extends U ? X : Y. They enable type-level logic that selects types based on conditions, similar to if-else at the type level.

faq.question: What are mapped types and when should I use them?
faq.answer: Mapped types iterate over property keys to transform existing types. Use them for creating variants like Partial, Readonly, or custom transformations that apply uniformly across all properties.

faq.question: How can I test my TypeScript types?
faq.answer: Create type assertion helpers that verify expected types at compile time. Use patterns like AssertEqual<T, U> that resolve to true for matching types. Test edge cases and ensure type errors occur where expected.

faq.question: What are template literal types used for?
faq.answer: Template literal types create string-based types with pattern matching. Use them for building type-safe string concatenation, path builders, event names, and enforcing consistent naming conventions.
